title: Address to the 78th Session of the United Nations General Assembly
titleOriginal: Discurso na Abertura da 78a Sessao da Assembleia Geral das Nacoes Unidas
occasion: 78th Session of the United Nations General Assembly

paragraphs.text: Mr. President of the General Assembly, Mr. Secretary-General, Heads of State and Government, ladies and gentlemen. It is with deep emotion that I return to this rostrum. Brazil is back on the world stage, and we are back with the conviction that multilateralism is the only path capable of addressing the immense challenges of our time.

When I last addressed this Assembly, many years ago, I spoke of a world divided between those who eat and those who go hungry. I am saddened to say that this fracture has not healed. It has deepened. Today, 735 million people face chronic hunger while food production has never been greater. This is not a failure of capacity. It is a failure of political will and a failure of the international order to distribute the fruits of human progress.

Brazil has placed the fight against hunger and poverty at the center of its domestic agenda and its international engagement. In my first months back in office, we relaunched the Bolsa Familia program and expanded it to reach 21 million families. We have revived the Zero Hunger strategy and are working to ensure that by the end of my term, no Brazilian will go without three meals a day. This is not charity. This is the most fundamental obligation of any government.

But hunger is a global scourge that demands a global response. That is why Brazil has proposed, and will champion during our G20 presidency next year, a Global Alliance Against Hunger and Poverty. We invite every nation represented here to join this effort. We have the technology, the agricultural knowledge, and the financial resources to end hunger in our lifetime. What we lack is the collective determination to act.

The Amazon rainforest is not merely a Brazilian asset. It is a planetary heritage. And I want to be very clear before this Assembly: Brazil will not accept being lectured about the Amazon by nations that destroyed their own forests centuries ago. But neither will we allow the destruction that accelerated in recent years to continue. In the first eight months of my government, deforestation in the Amazon fell by 48 percent. We are proving that economic development and environmental protection are not contradictory goals.

We will host the COP30 climate conference in Belem, in the heart of the Amazon, in 2025. This will be an opportunity for the world to see with its own eyes the forest we are fighting to preserve. But preservation requires resources. The developed world made promises in Paris and Glasgow. It is time to fulfill them. The 100 billion dollars a year pledged for climate finance is insufficient, and even that modest target has not been consistently met.

The climate crisis does not respect borders or wealth. The floods, droughts, and extreme weather events devastating communities across every continent make this painfully clear. But the burden falls most heavily on those who contributed least to the problem. Small island states, African nations, the poorest communities in Latin America — they are paying the price for an industrial model they did not create and from which they did not benefit.

I must speak frankly about the architecture of global governance. The United Nations Security Council, in its current form, does not reflect the world of 2023. It reflects the world of 1945. It is unacceptable that Africa, home to 1.4 billion people, has no permanent seat. It is unacceptable that Latin America and the Caribbean, with 650 million people, have no permanent representation. Reform of the Security Council is not a matter of prestige. It is a matter of legitimacy and effectiveness.

The same is true of the international financial architecture. The Bretton Woods institutions were designed for a world that no longer exists. The IMF and the World Bank must be reformed to give developing nations a greater voice and greater access to development finance. When a country faces a climate disaster, it should not be forced to choose between rebuilding its schools and servicing its debt to international creditors.

The war in Ukraine has caused enormous suffering. Brazil has consistently called for dialogue and a negotiated peace. War produces only destruction, grief, and instability. We do not accept the violation of territorial sovereignty, and we do not believe that escalation serves anyone’s interests. The international community must work to create the conditions for peace, not to prolong conflict.

At the same time, we cannot ignore that this conflict has exposed the double standards of the international system. Sanctions imposed without Security Council authorization affect the economies of developing nations that had no part in this war. Food and fertilizer prices spiked, and it was the poorest countries that suffered most. The weaponization of the global financial system is a dangerous precedent that undermines trust in international institutions.

Brazil believes in the power of South-South cooperation. Our partnerships with Africa, with Asia, with our Latin American neighbors are not alternatives to our relationships with developed nations. They are complementary. The BRICS grouping, which has now expanded, represents a desire not for confrontation but for a more balanced global order. We do not seek to replace existing institutions. We seek to ensure they serve all of humanity, not just a privileged few.

Technology offers immense promise, but it also carries risks of deepening inequality. Artificial intelligence, if governed properly, can accelerate development and improve lives. If left entirely to market forces and concentrated in the hands of a few corporations, it will entrench existing power imbalances. The governance of new technologies must be inclusive, transparent, and centered on the public interest.

I want to address the question of democracy. I know this subject well. I was imprisoned for 580 days on politically motivated charges. I was denied the right to run in the 2018 election. And yet I am here, elected by the Brazilian people, because democracy, when given the chance, corrects its own injustices. The insurrection of January 8th in Brasilia showed that the enemies of democracy have not disappeared. But the swift and institutional response showed that our democracy is resilient.

The digital platforms that connect us also carry disinformation that poisons public discourse and threatens democratic institutions around the world. We must address this challenge without compromising freedom of expression. Brazil is working on a regulatory framework that protects citizens from manipulation while preserving the open exchange of ideas. I invite other nations to join in this effort, because disinformation respects no borders.

Brazil’s economy is recovering. Inflation is falling, employment is rising, and investment is returning. We are demonstrating that a government can be fiscally responsible while also investing in its people. The new fiscal framework we have established provides predictability for markets while ensuring that social programs are not sacrificed on the altar of austerity. Growth with inclusion is not only possible — it is the only sustainable path.

Our reindustrialization agenda is designed for the twenty-first century. We are investing in the green economy, in renewable energy, in sustainable agriculture. Brazil already has one of the cleanest energy matrices in the world, with more than 80 percent of our electricity coming from renewable sources. We intend to expand this advantage and become a global leader in the energy transition, including in green hydrogen production.

To the young people of Brazil and of the world, I say this: do not lose faith in politics. Politics, when practiced with integrity and purpose, transforms lives. I am living proof of this. A boy who grew up in poverty in the northeast of Brazil, who could not read until the age of ten, stands before you as the president of the largest country in Latin America. This is the promise of democracy and the promise of a just society.

Brazil is ready to assume its responsibilities on the global stage. As president of the G20 in 2024, we will work to build consensus on the issues that matter most: hunger, inequality, climate, and the reform of global governance. We will do so through dialogue, through respect for sovereignty, and through the firm belief that no nation can solve these challenges alone.

I conclude by reaffirming Brazil’s unwavering commitment to peace, to multilateralism, and to the dignity of every human being. The world does not need more walls. It needs more bridges. It does not need more weapons. It needs more schools. Let us build, together, the future that our children deserve. Thank you very much.
